summary: Gruber reviews the M2 MacBook Air as a near-perfect realization of Apple's decades-long quest to build the ideal everyday laptop. He argues that Apple silicon has eliminated the compromises that plagued x86 laptops — heat, fan noise, poor battery life — and that most people, even current MacBook Pro users, would be better served by this thin, fanless Air. He addresses skepticism about fanless performance head-on, attributing it to years of bad experiences with Intel chips rather than any actual shortcoming of Apple silicon. He critiques some base-model cost-cutting and wishes for 11-inch and 15-inch size options, but concludes this is the closest Apple has come to a perfect laptop for nearly everyone.
key_points: The M2 MacBook Air is thinner, lighter, faster, and better in every regard than the M1 Air, with no meaningful tradeoffs
The 13-inch M2 MacBook Pro exists mainly because people scarred by Intel laptops can't believe a fanless Air could meet their needs — but for most users, the Air is the better choice
The failed 12-inch MacBook wasn't proof that thin fanless laptops are impossible — it was proof they're impossible on x86 architecture specifically
Apple's conservative color choices for the Air may reflect a deliberate design philosophy that vibrant colors work on phones and tablets but distract on laptops
The base $1,200 model has notable compromises (slower SSD, fewer GPU cores, older charger), making the $1,500 configuration the true default
money_quotes: Switching to the new M2 MacBook Air would be the biggest upgrade in their computing lives.
A thin, fan-less, high-performance laptop was and remains an impossible dream for the x86 computing architecture.

Trying to convince someone who's never actually lived with an M1 (or, now, M2) Mac just how much better the Apple silicon platform is than x86 is like trying to convince a time traveller from the 18th century how great indoor plumbing is.
In the first two years of the Apple silicon era of Macs, the rest of the industry has not only failed to close the gap, they've fallen even further behind.
It's taken decades of iterative refinement to get to this point: a nearly perfect laptop for nearly everyone.
In my daily use, this $1,900 MacBook Air feels identical to my $4,700 MacBook Pro.
Apple's MacBook hinge team does not get enough credit.
key_insight: Apple silicon has finally made the fanless, thin, high-performance laptop possible — something that was never an engineering failure of ambition but an inherent limitation of x86 architecture — and the M2 MacBook Air is the closest Apple has come to the ideal everyday computer.
